coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots decorated NiCo hydroxycarbonate hierarchical nanoarrays on carbon cloth with high areal capacitance as pseudocapacitor electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Wansheng
Zeng, Weiliang
Quan, Hongying
Pan, Menghua
Wang, Yunying
Chen, Dezhi
coredata.dc:description: The electrode materials with low specific capacitance seriously restricts the development of supercapacitors. Herein, CDs modified NiCo hydroxycarbonate (NiCoHC/CDs) hierarchical nanoarrays were grown on carbon cloth (CC) through a simple step by step hydrothermal method. The morphology, crystalline structure and electrochemical performance of the as-prepared NiCoHC nanoarrays on CC can be readily manipulated by varying the concentration of CDs in reaction solution. The optimized NiCoHC/CDs-2 nanoarrays on CC showed a high areal specific capacitance of 14.83 F cm‐2 (1964.64 F g‐1) at a current density of 5 mA cm‐2, excellent rate performance with a high capacitance retention of 46.8% at 50 mA cm‐2 and a good cycle stability with capacitance retention of 76.1% after 3000 cycles at 20 mA cm‐2. Furthermore, an asymmetric aqueous supercapacitor, which assembled using NiCoHC/CDs-2 as cathode and oxidized carbon cloth as anode in 6 M KOH, delivered a specific capacitance of 2.23 F cm‐2 (18.58 F cm‐3) in a potential window of 0‐1.5 V. An energy density of 5.82 mWh cm‐3 can be obtained at the power density of 33.58 mW cm‐3 and maintained 1.90 mWh cm‐3 at 98.99 mW cm‐3. Moreover, after 2000 cycles at 20 mA cm‐2, the device still retained 69.8% capacitance retention.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
Electrode materials
Nanoarrays
Hierarchical structure
Nanocomposites

National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of NiCoHC and CDs modified NiCoHC hierarchical nanoarrays growth on CC.
XRD patterns of NiCoHC nanoarrays with different volume of CDs modification on CC, a) NiCoHC, b) NiCoHC/CDs-1, c) NiCoHC/CDs-2 and d) NiCoHC/CDs-3.
Typical SEM images of a, b) NiCoHC, c, d) NiCoHC/CDs-1, e,f)NiCoHC/CDs-2 and g, h) NiCoHC/CDs-3 nanoarray on CC.

a) TEM image of NiCoHC/CDs-2 nanoarrays, b) HRTEM image of nanoneedle (Area I) c) HRTEM enlarged image of nanoflake (Area II), d) HAADF-STEM image and the corresponding EDS mapping images of NiCoHC/CDs-2 nanoarray, e) cobalt, f) nickel, g) oxygen, h) carbon.
XPS spectrum of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs-2. a) C1s, b) O1s, c) Ni2p and d) Co2p.

a) CV curves of the NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs electrodes at 5 mV s‐1; b) GCD curves of the as-prepared NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs electrodes at 5 mA cm‐2; c) CV curves of NiCoHC/CDs-2 electrode at different scan rates; d) CV curves of NiCoHC/CDs-2 electrode at different scan rates; e) calculated specific capacitance of the as-prepared NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CD electrodes at different current densities; f) long-term cycling stability of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs-2 electrode at 20 mA cm‐2.

Electrochemical properties of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs. a) CVs curves at different potential windows, b) CVs curves at different scan rates, c) GCD curves at different current densities, d) specific capacitance calculated from discharge curves at different current densities, e) Ragone plots, f ) GCD cycling stability at a current density of 20 mA cm‐2.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots decorated NiCo hydroxycarbonate hierarchical nanoarrays on carbon cloth with high areal capacitance as pseudocapacitor electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electrode materials with low specific capacitance seriously restricts the development of supercapacitors. Herein, CDs modified NiCo hydroxycarbonate (NiCoHC/CDs) hierarchical nanoarrays were grown on carbon cloth (CC) through a simple step by step hydrothermal method. The morphology, crystalline structure and electrochemical performance of the as-prepared NiCoHC nanoarrays on CC can be readily manipulated by varying the concentration of CDs in reaction solution. The optimized NiCoHC/CDs-2 nanoarrays on CC showed a high areal specific capacitance of 14.83 F cm‐2 (1964.64 F g‐1) at a current density of 5 mA cm‐2, excellent rate performance with a high capacitance retention of 46.8% at 50 mA cm‐2 and a good cycle stability with capacitance retention of 76.1% after 3000 cycles at 20 mA cm‐2. Furthermore, an asymmetric aqueous supercapacitor, which assembled using NiCoHC/CDs-2 as cathode and oxidized carbon cloth as anode in 6 M KOH, delivered a specific capacitance of 2.23 F cm‐2 (18.58 F cm‐3) in a potential window of 0‐1.5 V. An energy density of 5.82 mWh cm‐3 can be obtained at the power density of 33.58 mW cm‐3 and maintained 1.90 mWh cm‐3 at 98.99 mW cm‐3. Moreover, after 2000 cycles at 20 mA cm‐2, the device still retained 69.8% capacitance retention.

ce:section.ce:para:
With the rapid development of energy consumption and technology, the demand for high-performance energy storage equipment and technology is increasingly urgent [1]. As one of the emerging energy storage devices, supercapacitors have gradually become a research focus in recent years [2–4]. However, the energy density of the current supercapacitors is too low and cannot meet the growing demand for efficient energy storage [5–7]. According to the
E
=
C
( ∆ V )
2
2
, the key factors for improving energy density are the capacitance of electrode and working potential range of supercapacitor. Therefore, it is essential to select a suitable electrode material with high specific capacitance.

Owing to the charge storage mechanism of the redox reaction at the surface/subsurface layer, the specific capacitance of pseudocapacitor materials is remarkable higher than the carbon-containing electric double layers (EDLs) materials [8,9]. Among those pseudocapacitor materials, transitional metal carbonate hydroxides have shown great potential as electrode materials for supercapacitors due to their high specific capacitance and high hydrophilicity [10–12]. However, certain intrinsic defects such as lower electronic conductivity and volume change effects always lead to short life cycles and poor rate performance [13]. Construct hierarchical structures using transition metal carbonate hydroxide modified with conductive carbon material is one of the most effective approach to overcome autologous disadvantages of transitional metal carbonate hydroxide [14–17]. Many carbon materials, such as graphene [14,17], carbon nanotube [16,18,19], and activated carbon [15] have been used to modify and improve the specific surface area, surface wettability, and electrical conductivity of metal carbonate hydroxide.

Compared with the above carbon nanomaterials, zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) possess not only unique quantum confinement effects and edge effects [20], but also many advantages including good water solubility, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, wide source of raw materials, low cost and good biocompatibility [21,22]. Additionally, the good distribution of CDs in matrix will provide excellent interface between electrolytes and electrodes, which can improve the electrochemical performance of electrode materials [23,24]. For example, the reported CDs/NiCo2O4 [13,25], CDs/RuO2 [26], CDs/Fe3O4 [27], CDs/Ni(OH)2 [28,29], CDs/V2O5 [30], etc. have showed profound improvement in specific capacitance, rate capability and cycling stability in comparison of bare metal oxides/hydroxides. Although CDs are widely concerned in metal oxides and hydroxides, CDs modified metal carbonate hydroxide has not been reported in supercapacitor until now.

Herein, a simple step by step hydrothermal strategy was used to fabricate CDs decorated bimetallic NiCo hydroxycarbonate (NiCoHC/CDs) hierarchical nanoarrays on carbon cloth (CC). Owing to the oxygen-rich surface and high hydrophilicity of CDs, the morphology of the prepared NiCoHC hierarchical nanoarrays can be controlled by adjusting the concentration of CDs during the reaction. The optimized NiCoHC/CDs sample can release a high areal capacitance up to 14.83 F cm‐2 (1964.64 F g‐1) at a current density of 5 mA cm‐2 and maintain 6.94 F cm‐2 (919.47 F g‐1) at a high current density of 50 mA cm‐2. Furthermore, an asymmetric supercapacitor was fabricated by using NiCoHC/CDs on CC as cathode and oxidized CC as anode in 6 M KOH. The assembled devices exhibited a high specific capacitance of 2.23 F cm‐2 (18.58 F cm‐3) with a high energy density of 5.82 mWh cm‐3 (332.95 Wh kg‐1). Moreover, the constructed device retained 69.8% capacitance retention after 2000 cycles at a current density of 20 mA cm‐2. The results showed that the decoration of CDs can effectively improve the electrochemical performance of NiCoHC nanoarrays.

All reagents are of analytical grade and can be used without further purification. CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method [31,32]. Briefly, citric acid (3.0 g) and urea (1.0 g) were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water by sonication for 2 h. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained black solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 60 min to remove large particles.

NiCoHC/CDs was prepared by a two-step hydrothermal method. First, a certain amount of CDs solution (14 mg/ mL- 1) was mixed with 2.85 g of nickel chloride hexahydrate, 2.88 g of urea, 0.89 g of ammonium fluoride, and diluted to 80 mL with deionized water. The solution was continuously stirred for 0.5 h. Then, the reaction solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing a piece of cleaned CC (4 cm × 4 cm). The autoclave was then sealed and held at 120 °C for 6 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the obtained NiHC/CDs on CC was washed using deionized water and absolute ethanol several times and dried in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h. Second, a certain amount CDs solution (14 mg/mL) and 1.75 g of cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, 1.80 g of urea and 0.56 g of ammonium fluoride were mixed together, and diluted to 80 mL with deionized water, and then stirred for 0.5 h to fully dissolve, the resulting solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The as-prepared NiHC/CDs grown on CC in first step were immersed in the solution, and then the autoclave was sealed and held at 120 °C for 9 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained NiCoHC/CDs on CC was rinsed with deionized water and absolute ethanol, and dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The volume of CDs solution used in the above two steps were 1, 5 and 10 mL, respectively, the corresponding NiCoHC/CDs were named as NiCoHC/CDs-1, NiCoHC/CDs-2, NiCoHC/CDs-3, respectively. In addition, the bare NiCoHC was prepared using the same two-step method without CDs. The mass loading of NiCoHC, NiCoHC/CDs-1, NiCoHC/CDs-2, NiCoHC/CDs-3 on CC was 10.37, 9.04, 7.55, 7.68 mg cm‐2, respectively.

The phase of the as-prepared samples was measured by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Cu K
a radiation λ = 1.5406 Å) on a XRD diffractometer. The morphology and structure of the as-prepared samples were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Quanta 250) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200X) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy detector (EDS). The elements and chemical states on the surface of the as-prepared samples were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Axis Ultra). The pore structure of the as-prepared samples were analyzed by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm at 77 K obtained on surface area & pore size analyzer (Micromeritics TriStar II 3020), and the samples were desorbed in advance at 120 °C for 10 h. Oxygen defect of the as-prepared samples were evaluated by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra collected on a Bruker A300 EPR spectrometer.

Electrochemical properties of the as-prepared samples were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) on a CHI760e electrochemical workstation using three-electrode system in 6 M KOH aqueous solution at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C). Here, nanoarrays modified CC (1 cm × 1 cm), platinum plate (1 cm × 1 cm) and Hg/HgO electrode were used as the work electrode, the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively.

The quasi-reversible Faradic reactions in the electrode makes the GCD curve of the samples asymmetric, and the charge/voltage ratio of Faradic reactions no longer remains constant, but varies with time, thus the areal special capacitance (C
s ) of the as-prepared samples in three-electrode system were calculated according to the Eq. (1) [33], the galvanostatic specific capacitance was calculated according to the Eq. (2), respectively.
(1)
C
s
=
2
i
s
×
∫
Vdt
V
2
V
i
V
f
(2)
C
m
=
C
s
× S
m
Where C s (F cm‐2) represents the areal special capacitance, i s = I/S (A cm‐2) is the current density, I (A) is the current, S (cm2) is the area of the working electrode,
∫
Vdt
(V·s) is the integral current area, V (V) is the potential with initial and final values of V i (V) and V f (V), respectively.
Where C m (F g‐1) represents the galvanostatic specific capacitance, m (g) is the mass loading of the working electrode.

In addition, the electrochemical performances of the as-prepared samples were also estimated in a full-cell configuration, an asymmetry supercapacitor device (ASC) was assembled using the NiCoHC/CDs grown on CC as cathode, the oxidized CC (Section S1, Supporting information) as anode and a piece of filter paper as separator as well as 6 M KOH aqueous solution as electrolyte. The device was sealed by PVC film, and the thickness of the devices was 1.2 mm. Electrochemical properties of the device were assessed on a CHI760e electrochemical workstation and a CT2001A supercapacitance test system at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C). The energy density and power density of devices were calculated through the following Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively [33,34].
(3)
E =
I
∫
Vdt
3.6 V
(4)
P =
E × 3600
t
where E is the energy density (mWh cm‐3), I (A) is the current, V (cm‐3) is the total volume of both electrodes,
∫
Vdt
(V·s) is the galvanostatic discharge current area, P is the power density (mW cm‐3), t is discharge time (s), respectively.

The typical TEM and HRTEM morphology of as-prepared CDs are shown in Fig. S1a–b. The diameter of as-prepared CDs is mainly distributed range from 2 to 12 nm. A well-defined fringe with lattice spacing of 0.208 nm can be observed (Fig. S1b), corresponding to the (101) plane of graphic carbon. Due to the abundant oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups (Fig. S1c), the as-prepared CDs can be well-dispersed in the aqueous solution. Two weak diffraction peaks at 26° and 44° in the XRD pattern (Fig. S1d) further confirms the successful preparation of CDs. As shown inset of Fig. S1a, the yellow solution of CDs solution emits bright blue FL under 360 nm UV light excitation. On account of their distinguishing feature of small size, containing functional groups and graphitization, CDs are expected to regulate the growth and crystallization of nanomaterials.

Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram of procedure to prepare the NiCoHC and CDs modified NiCoHC hierarchical nanoarrays through two-steps method. The NiHC nanoplates were grown on CC in the first step through hydrothermal synthesis, and the CoHC nanoneedles grew on the NiHC nanoplates in the second step. For NiCoHC/CDs, CDs modified NiHC (NiHC/CDs) nanosheets were grown on CC by a hydrothermal process at first step, and then CDs modified CoHC were uniformly grown on the surface of NiHC/CDs nanoarrays at second step, and formed the NiCoHC/CDs hierarchical arrays structure on CC finally. Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of NiCoHC/CDs with different CDs loading on CC. The obvious diffraction peaks at 2θ = 17.5°, 26.7°, 30.7° 33.9°, 35.4° 36.5°, 39.7°, 47.2° and 59.9° match well with the (020), (220), (300), (221), (040), (301), (231), (340) and (412) planes of Co(CO3)0.5(OH)H2O (PDF#48-0083), respectively. The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 18.7°, 24.1°, 32.4° 34.9°, 35.3°, 59.6° and 60.2° agree well with the (200), (130), (320), (‐201), (201) and (002/006) and (521) planes Ni2(CO3)(OH)2 (PDF#35-0501). Notably, the shift to low angle for the diffraction peak of (200) may be attributed to the Co doping in the Ni2(CO3)(OH)2. In addition, two diffraction peaks at 2θ = 18.7° and 38.9° may be attributed to the (‐102) and (‐223) planes Ni5(CO3)4(OH)24.5H2O (PDF#46-1398). With the increase of CDs loading, the crystallinity of as-prepared NiCoHC/CDs nanoarrays decreased, indicating that the CDs modification can weaken the crystallinity of NiCoHC nanoarrays. Moreover, we can observe a bread-like peak at 25°, which corresponds to the carbon cloth.

SEM was used to analyze the morphology and microstructure of NiCoHC/CDs nanoarrays, the obtained typical SEM images are shown in Fig. 3. The results show that NiCoHC/CDs are generally comprise of nanoarrays with different surface structures, and the morphologies of samples rely on the content of CDs in synthesis. When no CDs were incorporated, needle-like CoHC nanoarrays (Fig. 3a and b) can be observed on the surface of flake-like NiHC nanoarrays (Fig. S2a–b) after two-steps hydrothermal process. When a small volume of CDs was added (1 mL) in the reaction solution, the size of CoHC nanoneedles on the surface of flake-like NiHC nanoarrays was reduced in the prepared NiCoHC/CDs-1, as shown in Fig. 3c and d. When the volume of CDs was increased to 5 mL, the size of CoHC nanoneedles was further decreased (Fig. 3e and f) in the as-prepared NiCoHC/CDs-2. When the volume of the CDs increased to 10 mL, the CoHC nanoneedles in the NiCoHC/CDs-3 was transformed into flocculate (Fig. 3h), and the resulting NiCoHC/CDs-3 looks like dandelion finally. The above SEM analysis indicates that the introduction of CDs in the growth of NiHC and CoHC nanocrystals can induce the transformation from the array structure to flocculate structure, resulting in the decrease of crystallinity. To confirm the role of CDs, we further explored the effect of CDs on the morphology of materials in different reaction processes. The typical SEM images of NiHC and NiHC/CDs-2 are presented in Fig. S2a–b and S2c–d, it can be observed that the NiHC nanoplates prepared without CDs at first step have a regular arrangement and the surface of nanoplate was smooth (Fig. S2a and b), but the surface of NiHC/CDs-2 nanosheets became rougher (Fig. S2c and d) and the thickness of NiHC/CDs-2 nanosheets became thinner with the induction of CDs. As shown in Fig. S2e and f, the obtained CoHC nanocrystals at second step were still needle-like structure on the surface of NiHC/CDs-2 without the presence of CDs. Therefore, it can be inferred that the morphological changes of NiCoHC/CDs hierarchical structures are attributed to the adding of CDs, which act as effective surfactants to induce the growth of crystal due to the abundant groups on the surface of carbon dots.

Taking NiCoHC/CDs-2 as examples, the detailed microstructures of the as-prepared samples were further investigated by TEM. Fig. 4a displays a typical TEM image of NiCoHC/CDs-2 nanoarrays removed from the surface of CC. It can be clearly observed that the CoHC nanoneedles grew on the curly NiHC nanosheets and formed the hierarchical arrays structure. Fig. 4b shows the HRTEM image of the CoHC nanoneedle (Area I in Fig. 4a). The clear lattice fringes with the lattice spacing of 2.5 Å can be observed in the middle of CoHC nanoneedle, which matches well with the (040) plane of Co(CO3)0.5(OH)H2O (PDF#48-0083). In addition, at the edge CoHC nanoneedle, some indistinct lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 3.3 Å are attributed to (002) plane of CDs, which reveals that CDs are well combined with CoHC nanoneedle. Fig. 4c shows the HRTEM image of the curly NiHC nanosheets (Area II in Fig. 4a). Lots of nanospheres with ~5 nm in diameter were evenly dispersed on the surface of nanosheets. The clear lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 2.12 and 2.57 Å match well with the (132) plane of Ni5(CO3)4(OH)24.5H2O (PDF#46-1398) and (‐201) plane of Ni2(CO3)(OH)2 (PDF#35-0501), respectively. Furthermore, the CDs lattice fringes with spacing of 3.3 Å also can be seen at the edge of nanosheets. The CDs with graphitized lattice are mainly observed at the edge of the NiCoHC/CDs nanoarrays, indicating that CDs mainly locate on the surface of the nanoarrays and fill in the gaps between NiHC nanosheet and CoHC nanoneedle. Such hierarchical structure makes CDs adequately contact with the electrolyte when used as the electrode materials, and the oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups on CDs can also contribute to the pseudocapacitance and promote the rapid transfer of charge carriers during the charge-discharge processes.

Fig. 4d shows the HAADF-STEM image of NiCoHC/CDs-2, the crimped morphology of NiHC nanoflake induced by the CDs are further observed. The presented flocculent structure for the NiCoHC/CDs-2 and the acute edge of nanoneedles for the NiCoHC further confirm the induction effect of CDs in the growth of NiCoHC, which can effectively improve the porous structure and increase the defect of nanocrystals. The corresponding EDS mapping images (Fig. 4e‐h) show that cobalt, nickel, oxygen and carbon elements are uniformly dispersed in both nanoneedles and nanoflakes, indicating that the mutual doping of metal ions in as-prepared materials. According to TG curves (Fig. S3) of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs-2, the mass of modified CDs on NiCoHC/CDs-2 is about 9.0%.

The porous structure of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs were evaluated by N2 adsorption/desorption measurement at 77 K. Fig. S4a displays the obtained N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of NiCoHC, NiCoHC/CDs-1, NiCoHC/CDs-2 and NiCoHC/CDs-3, which can be identified as quasi type IV. The distinct H3 hysteresis loop in the P/P0 range of 0.3‐1.0 indicates the presence of mesopore in NiCoHC, NiCoHC/CDs-1, NiCoHC/CDs-2and NiCoHC/CDs-3, which are original from the accumulation of flake-like particles. The obviously bigger hysteresis loop of NiCoHC/CDs-2 than other samples suggest the more abundant mesopores in NiCoHC/CDs-2. According to the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation, the calculated specific surface area (SBET) of NiCoHC, NiCoHC/CDs-1, NiCoHC/CDs-2 and NiCoHC/CDs-3 are 26.8, 35.3, 62.9 and 48.1 m2 g‐1 −1, respectively ( Table 1). The pore size distribution curves (inset Fig. S4a) of NiCoHC, NiCoHC/CDs-1, NiCoHC/CDs-2, and NiCoH/CD-3 calculated from the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method confirmed the mesoporous nature of the samples. We can observe two main pore size centered at 3.8 and 34.6 nm for NiCoHC as well as a wide distribution around 20 nm for NiCoHC/CDs-2. The total pore volume of NiCoHC, NiCoHC/CDs-1, NiCoHC/CDs-2 and NiCoHC/CDs-3 are 0.11, 0.14, 0.17 and 0.10 m3 g‐1, respectively (Table 1). The higher SBET and pore volume implies the higher active sites and more convenient channel of NiCoHC/CDs-2 for energy storage. Furthermore, the more mesopores in NiCoHC/CDs-2 can effectively reduce the diffusion resistance of electrolyte and accelerate the ion migration of electrode materials. ERP was used to further analyze the defect of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs-2. As shown in Fig. S4b, a signal at g = 2.002 corresponding to the oxygen vacancy [35,36]. The increased intensity for NiCoHC/CDs-2 indicates more oxygen vacancy after CDs modification, which would be beneficial to improve the electrochemical performance of electrode materials.

In order to obtain more information about the surface composition and chemical state of NiCoHC after CDs modification, the XPS spectra of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs-2 were obtained and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 5. The XPS survey spectra of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs-2 (Fig. S5) demonstrate the existence of Ni, Co, O, C and F elements. The F elements was originated from ammonium fluoride. Fig. 5a displays the high resolution C1s spectra of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs-2. For the NiCoHC, the C1s spectra can be divided into three species, including C‐C (284.6 eV), C‐O (286.1 eV and C═O in CO3 (289.0 eV). For the NiCoHC/CDs-2, apart from the C‐C, C‐O and C═O peaks at 284.6, 286.2 and 289.5 eV, respectively, a peak centered at 281.4 eV can be attributed to metal-C bonds (Ni/Co‐C), which indicates that the introduction of CDs can induce the formation of metal-C bonds. The presence of metal-C bonds may effectively improve the conductive of NiCo hydroxycarbonate hierarchical nanoarrays. Fig. 5b exhibits the O1s spectra of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs-2. The O1s spectra of NiCoHC show two signals at 530.1 and 530.9 eV, corresponding to the O in the CO3 and OH, respectively. After modification with CDs, a new peak at 532.8 eV in O1s spectra of the NiCoHC/CDs-2 can be fitted into C‒O from CDs. Fig. 5c demonstrates the Ni 2p spectra of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs-2. Two main peaks centered at 855 and 873 eV correspond to the Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2 respectively. In addition, two satellite peaks centered at 861 and 879 eV indicates the presence of Ni2+ in as-prepared NiCoHC samples. For the bare NiCoHC, the Ni 2p3/2 XPS spectra can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 854.9 and 856.3 eV, corresponding to the Ni(OH)2 and NiCO3 [37], respectively. After modification with CDs, the Ni 2p3/2 spectra of NiCoHC/CDs-2 can be fitted to three peaks at 854.7, 856.6 and 858.1 eV, which are attributed to the Ni-OH, Ni-CO3 [37] and Ni-C bond [38], respectively. Similarly, the Co 2p spectra (Fig. 5d) exhibits two peaks centered at 780.5 and 796.5 eV with their corresponding satellite peaks at 785.5 and 801.6 eV, which are attributed to the Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively. The Co 2p3/2 spectrum of NiCoHC can be divided two parts centered at 780.0 and 781.9 eV, corresponding to Co‐OH and Co‐CO [37], respectively. Furthermore, the Co 2p3/2 spectrum of NiCoHC/CDs-2 can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 779.7, 781.4 and 782.5 eV, indicating the presence of Co(OH)2, CoCO3 [37] and Co‐C bond. The presence of Co‐C and Ni‐C bond further confirmed the presence of metal-C bonds in the obtained NiCoHC/CDs-2 composite nanoarrays, which can be propitious to ameliorate the electrochemical properties of NiCoHC/CDs-2.

The electrochemical properties of the as-prepared NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs were tested in three-electrode system, the results are exhibited in Fig. 6. Fig. 6a shows the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs electrodes at a scan rate of 5 mV s‐1 in a voltage window of 0‐0.6 V. The obviously redox peaks suggest the pseudocapacitive characteristics of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs electrodes, and the corresponding capacitance is mainly originated from the reversible reaction of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+. Compare with NiCoHC, the larger current density response of NiCoHC/CDs electrodes at the same scan rate indicates a higher capacitance than the NiCoHC electrode. It suggests that the modification with CDs can effectively increase the capacitance of NiCoHC, and the NiCoHC/CDs-2 showed the highest capacitance among all as-prepared samples. Fig. 6b displays the GCD curves of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs at the current density of 5 mA cm‐2 in the potential window of 0 - 0.5 V (vs. Hg/HgO). The clear potential plateaus further indicate the redox reactions of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+ in the pseudocapacitance contribution of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs. The NiCoHC/CDs electrodes possessed the longer discharge time than the bare NiCoHC electrode, which is consistent with the CV results. In addition, the NiCoHC/CDs-2 delivered the highest discharge time in comparison with other electrodes, suggesting the best capacitance performance. To clarify the capacitance contribution of CDs in NiCoHC/CDs electrodes, the electrochemical properties of CDs modified CC using the same preparation approach was investigated by CV method. Fig. S6 shows the CV curves of CDs (5 mL) modified CC in 6 M KOH, and the very low current responses imply the negligible contribution of CDs in the capacitance of NiCoHC/CDs electrodes.

Since NiCoHC/CDs-2 electrode exhibited superior capacitive performance than other samples, the electrochemical properties of NiCoHC/CDs-2 was further evaluated in detail. Fig. 6c shows the CV curves of NiCoHC/CDs-2 at different scan rates. It can be observed that the shape of CV curves distorted to some extent with the increasing of scan rate because of the common polarization generated during the rapid redox process in the surface of electrode. Fig. 6d reveals the GCD curves of NiCoHC/CDs-2 at different current densities, the symmetrical charge/discharge curves without distortion as the current density increased from 5 and 50 mA cm‐2, indicate the good capacitance behavior of NiCoHC/CDs-2. The areal capacitance of the as-prepared samples was calculated from the discharge curve (Figs. 6d and S7) and shown in Fig. 6e. The calculated areal capacitance of NiCoHC, NiCoHC/CDs-1, NiCoHC/CDs-2 and NiCoHC/CDs-3 electrodes were 3.33, 7.75, 14.83 and 10.96 F cm‐2 at 5 mA cm‐2, and the corresponding gravimetric capacitance were 320.73, 857.74, 1964.64, and 1427.60 F g‐1, respectively. The areal capacitance of NiCoHC/CDs-2 is nearly five times as much as NiCoHC. Even the current density increased to 50 mA cm‐2, the areal capacitance of NiCoHC, NiCoHC/CDs-1, NiCoHC/CDs-2 and NiCoHC/CDs-3 electrodes were 1.80, 4.78, 6.94, and 3.59 F cm‐2, and the corresponding gravimetric capacitance were 173.10, 528.76, 919.47, and 467.58 F g‐1, respectively, exhibits capacitance retention of 54.0%, 61.6%, 46.8% and 32.8%, respectively. The capacitance performance of NiCoHC/CDs is significantly better than NiCoHC, especially for NiCoHC/CDs-2. Long-term cycling stability is another important factor for electrode materials. The cycling stability of the NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs-2 electrode were evaluated by the repeating GCD cycle at a current density of 20 mA cm‐2 (Fig. 6f). After 3000 GCD cycles, the NiCoHC/CDs-2 electrode possessed a high capacitance retention of 76.1%, which is higher than that of NiCoHC (57%), suggesting that the addition of carbon dots can improve the long-term stability of the NiCoHC.

The excellent capacitance performance of NiCoHC/CDs is mainly attributed to the following reasons. Firstly, the modification of CDs on NiCoHC and the presence of metal-C bonds can effectively increase the conductivity of electrode and accelerate ion diffusion, which was confirmed by the EIS spectra of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs. As shown in Fig. S8a–b, the Nyquist plots of NiCoHC and NiCoHC/CDs contain a quasi-semicircle in high frequency region and a linear part in low frequency region. At high frequency region, the intercept to real axis corresponds the bulk resistances of the electrode electrochemical system, known as equivalent series resistance (R
s ), which restricts the output power of the supercapacitor, and is the main energy dissipation factor inside the supercapacitor. The diameter of semicircle represents the charge-transfer resistance (R
ct ) of the electrodes and electrolyte interface. Obviously, the R
s and R
ct of NiCoHC/CDs were lower than NiCoHC, and NiCoHC/CDs-2 has the lowest R
s and R
ct in all samples (Table S1). Furthermore, the slope of linear part denotes the in the low frequency diffusion resistances of ion diffusion/transport, the higher slope indicates the lower diffusion resistances of electrodes. As shown in Fig. S8a–b, NiCoHC/CDs has lower slope compare with NiCoHC. The much smaller charge-transfer resistance and lower diffusion resistances of NiCoHC/CDs indicate that CDs modification indeed improve the conductivity of NiCoHC, which is beneficial to the high capacitance performance. As shown in Fig. S8c–d, after 3000 cycles at 20 mA cm‐2, the R
s and R
ct of the electrodes were increased. This may be due to the falling of the active substances after long-term cycling, which leads to the deterioration of conductivity and the increase of interface resistance [39,40]. Secondly, the increased specific surface area and abundant functional groups of CDs modification provide more active sites for the energy storage. Thirdly, the hierarchical architecture of the NiCoHC after CDs modification facilitates ion diffusion and electron transport, which benefit to the rate performances of the composites. In comparison with NiCoHC (without CDs modification), the size of CoHC nanoneedles on the surface of flake-like NiHC nanoarrays was reduced gradually in NiCoHC/CDs-1 and NiCoHC/CDs-2, which can provide shorter path for the charge transfer, thus the NiCoHC/CDs-1 and NiCoHC/CDs-2 show better rate performances than NiCoHC. Furthermore, the good dispersibility and the rich surface functional groups of CDs not only ensure the evenness and stability of electrodes, but also improve the surface wettability of electrode materials, in turn to facilitate the electrochemical reactions between electrodes and electrolytes.

To evaluate the practical application of NiCoHC/CDs-2, the supercapacitor device was assembled using NiCoHC/CDs-2 as cathode and oxidized CC (OCC) as anode cathode in the electrolyte of 6 M KOH. The detail synthesis process of OCC was described in the Section S1(Supplementary Materials). The capacitance properties of OCC were shown in Fig. S9. As can be seen from the CV curves of OCC (Fig. S9a) that the capacitance of OCC electrode was mainly originated from the electric double layer of carbon materials. In addition, a pair of redox peaks at −0.4/−0.8 V vs. Hg/HgO in the CV curves may be attributed to the O-doped groups on the surface of OCC. Fig. S9b displays the GCD curves of OCC, the shape of quasi-symmetric triangle suggests the ideal capacitance properties of OCC. The areal capacitances of OCC calculated from the discharge curves (Fig. S9b) were 3.16, 2.95, 2.87, 2.80, 2.77, 2.74 and 2.70 F cm‐2 at the current densities of 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40 and 50 mA cm‐2, respectively. According to the charge balance, the area ratio of NiCoHC/CDs-2 and OCC is about 1:3.

Fig. 7 demonstrates the CV curves of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs tested at different potential window, the redox peaks of the CV curves suggesting a typical capacitive behavior for the assembled NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs. Furthermore, a stable potential window up to 1.5 V indicating a combined contribution from pseudocapacitive of NiCoHC/CDs-2 and electric double-layer capacitance from OCC. Fig. 7b shows the CV curves of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs at different scan rates. As the scan rate increased from 10 to 50 mV s‐1, a negligible deformation indicates the good rate capability of assembled ASCs. Fig. 7c displays the GCD curves of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs at various current densities. According to the discharge curves, the calculated specific capacitances of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs were shown in Fig. 7d. At the current density of 5 mA cm‐2, the areal capacitance and volumetric capacitance of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs was 2.23 F cm‐2 and 18.58 F cm‐3. More importantly, the NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs still delivered a high areal capacitance and a high volumetric capacitance of 0.73 F cm‐2 and 6.08 F cm‐3, respectively, even the current density was increased to 30 mA cm‐2, indicates that the NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs possessed high specific capacitance and good rate capability. Power density and energy density are the key evaluating indicator to estimate the performance of a supercapacitor. Fig. 7e exhibits the Ragone plots of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs. The energy density of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs was 5.82 mWh cm‐3 (332.95 Wh kg‐1) with the power density of 33.58 mW cm‐3 (1920.87 W kg‐1), and retained 1.90 mWh cm−3 (108.46 Wh kg‐1) at 98.99 mW cm‐3 (5650.59 W kg‐1), which are superior than the reported aqueous supercapacitors, such as 3D N-C@NiCo2S4 NWs//AC (1.72 mWh cm‐3/10.63 mW cm‐3) [41], Mn3O4-γ-MnOOH@CC (1.47 mWh cm‐3/19.3 mW cm‐3) [42], WO3@ACC//AC@ACC (0.5 mWh cm‐3/62 mW cm‐3) [43], NiO/NiOOH@NF//PPy@rECFC (4.12 mWh cm‐3/9.17 mW cm‐3) [44], α-Fe2O3/C/CC//MnO2/CC (0.64 mWh cm‐3/14.8 mW cm‐3) [45], PPy/FCC//MnO2/CC (0.8 mWh cm‐3/12.85 mW cm‐3) [46], MACC//Co(OH)2/CC (1.49 mWh cm‐3/19.45 mW cm‐3) [47], and Co3O4@Co-CH//AC/NF (0.03 mWh cm‐3/3.37 mW cm‐3) [48]. The cycling stability of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs was tested by repeating charge-discharge under the current density of 20 mA cm‐2 and the result was shown in Fig. 7f. After 2000 cycles, the capacitance retention of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs was gradually stabilized at 69.8%. The coulomb efficiency of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs was nearly 100%. Finally, in order to intuitively display the potential practical application of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs, two NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC ASCs devices connected in series can successfully light up different color light-emitting-diodes (LEDs, Fig. S10), and the corresponding video of lighting LED was shown in Video S1. Therefore, modified with CDs is an effectual strategy to regulate the microstructure and improve the electrochemical performance of electrode materials, and we believe that CDs will make great achievements in the field of electrochemical energy storage in the future.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.159048.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
.

In conclusion, a simple step-by-step hydrothermal method was used to prepare CDs modified NiCo bimetallic hydroxycarbonate hierarchical nanoarrays on CC (NiCoHC/CDs). As electrode for supercapacitor, the optimized NiCoHC/CDs-2 delivered significantly enhanced capacitive performance compared with pristine NiCoHC sample. It is mainly attributed the modification of CDs, which can regulate the morphology and provide abundant groups, resulting in increased specific surface area and defect as well as conductivity of as-prepared hierarchical nanoarrays, which are benefit to enhance the active sites and facilitate ion diffusion. Furthermore, the assembled asymmetry supercapacitors of NiCoHC/CDs-2//OCC released a high energy density of 5.82 mWh cm‐3 with the power density of 33.58 mW cm‐3. These impressive results demonstrate that the modification of CD by a simple step-by-step hydrothermal method is an effectively approach to fabricate high performance electrode materials for supercapacitors.

Wansheng Luo: Experimental design, Data curation, Writing - original draft preparation. Weiliang Zeng: Experimental design, Data curation, Writing - original draft preparation. Hongying Quan: Experimental design, Writing - reviewing and editing. Menghua Pan: Data curation. Yunying Wang: Supervision. Dezhi Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

.

Wu, 3D carbon coated NiCo2S4 nanowires doped with nitrogen for electrochemical energy storage and conversion, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 556 (2019) 449‐457.
